1:
On Numbers 29:1 — “And in the seventh month, on the first day of the month, ye shall have an holy convocation… it is a day of blowing the trumpets unto you” — the witnesses mark the feast of trumpets and the holy seventh month.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the feast of trumpets on the first day of the seventh month, with its sacrifices. The Puritan Henry: more sacred solemnities in the seventh month than in any other, it having once been the first month. The Reformed Gill: the month Tisri, famous for more religious days than any other. Calvin: not the festivals but the sacrifices are here treated; in the seventh month was the memorial of blowing of trumpets.

The verse opens the seventh-month feasts. The first day is a holy convocation, the day of blowing trumpets. The richest month of the sacred year begins.

So the witnesses see the chapter open with the feast of trumpets, the first of the great solemnities of the seventh month. The witnesses note the eminence of the month: “the seventh month” — Gill and Henry observe that this month (Tisri, answering to part of September and October) was the most full of holy days of any in the year, the trumpets, the day of atonement, and the feast of tabernacles all falling within it; Henry recalls that it had once been the first month, before the Exodus made Abib the head of the year, and so retained a special sacredness. They mark the rite of the day: “a day of blowing the trumpets” — the new moon of the seventh month was kept with an extraordinary blowing of trumpets, a memorial summoning the people to the solemn season now opening. They observe Calvin’s note on the scope of the chapter: that here it is not the festivals themselves that are treated (their institution being given in Leviticus), but only the sacrifices by which their solemnization was to be graced; the chapter sets out the offerings proper to each holy day. They note the purpose of the holy convocation, that the people were called together to worship at the threshold of the holiest month. They observe that the trumpets, sounding over the land, gathered Israel to the round of penitence, atonement, and joy that the month would bring. They note that God appointed both the day and its offerings. The verse thus opens the seventh-month feasts: the day of trumpets — its burnt offering following in the next verses.
2:
On Numbers 29:2 — “And ye shall offer a burnt offering for a sweet savour unto the LORD; one young bullock, one ram, and seven lambs of the first year without blemish” — the witnesses mark the burnt offering of the feast of trumpets.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: a burnt offering for a sweet savour — one young bullock, one ram, and seven lambs of the first year without blemish, with their meat offering of flour and oil. The Reformed Gill: the burnt offering appointed for the day.

The verse names the offering. A bullock, a ram, and seven unblemished lambs are offered. The day of trumpets is honoured with sacrifice.

So the witnesses see the burnt offering appointed for the feast of trumpets, ascending to God as a sweet savour. The witnesses note the victims: “one young bullock, one ram, and seven lambs of the first year without blemish” — the special burnt offering of the day, offered whole upon the altar; Keil and Delitzsch detail it with its accompanying meat offering of fine flour mingled with oil, three tenth deals for the bullock, two for the ram, and a tenth for each lamb. They mark the requirement of perfection: “without blemish” — every victim must be sound and unflawed, fit for God’s altar, as in all the offerings. They observe the end declared: “for a sweet savour unto the LORD” — the whole, wholly consumed and ascending in flame, was to be a fragrance well-pleasing to God, the day’s worship rising to him in the smoke of the burnt offering. They note that the trumpets summoned the people, and the burnt offering consecrated the day; the sound and the sacrifice together opened the holy season. They observe the typology the witnesses carry through all the offerings: the unblemished victims, wholly offered, point to Christ, who offered himself without spot to God (Hebrews 9:14), the true burnt offering of entire devotion. They note that the day of trumpets, like the other feasts, was honoured with the costliest token of worship. They observe that the offering was the same in kind as that of the atonement and the eighth day. The verse thus names the burnt offering — its meat offering specified in the next verse.
3:
On Numbers 29:3 — “And their meat offering shall be of flour mingled with oil, three tenth deals for the bullock, and two tenth deals for the ram” — the witnesses mark the meat offering of the day.

The Reformed Gill: the meat offering went along with the creatures of the burnt offering, the quantity of flour and oil the same as at the new moons and the feast of unleavened bread.

The verse sets the meat offering. Flour and oil in fixed measure accompany each victim. The grain offering attends the burnt offering.

So the witnesses see the meat offering of flour and oil appointed to accompany the burnt offering of the day. The witnesses note the proportions: “three tenth deals for the bullock, and two tenth deals for the ram” (and, as the next verse adds, a tenth for each lamb) — the meat offering was measured to the victim, the largest portion for the bullock, less for the ram, least for each lamb, all of fine flour mingled with oil. They mark Gill’s observation that this was the same quantity of flour and oil as in the meat offering at the new moons and at the feast of unleavened bread; the rule of the accompanying offering, fixed elsewhere, held here also, the grain offering always proportioned to the animal sacrifice. They observe the principle the witnesses have drawn out through these chapters: that the beast of the burnt offering was never offered alone, but with its meat offering of flour and oil, the fruits of the field joined to the firstlings of the flock and herd, that the whole produce of the land be brought to God together. They note the meaning the witnesses find: the fine flour and oil owned God as the giver of bread and of the oil of gladness, and (in the figure) pointed to Christ, the bread of life, anointed with the Spirit. They observe that the worship of the day was furnished in full, sacrifice and grain offering together. They note that God ordered even the measures with exactness. The verse thus sets the meat offering — the offering reckoned, in the next verses, besides the continual and new-moon sacrifices.
6:
On Numbers 29:6 — “Beside the burnt offering of the month, and his meat offering, and the daily burnt offering… for a sweet savour” — the witnesses mark the day’s offerings added to the new-moon and daily sacrifices.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: “the burnt offering of the month” is better “of the new moon” (Numbers 28:11). The Reformed Gill: the bullock, ram, and seven lambs of the feast were besides the new-moon offering and the continual daily burnt offering. Wesley: of the month, belonging to every new moon.

The verse adds upon the others. The feast offerings are besides the new-moon and the daily sacrifice. The day’s worship is heaped upon the standing offerings.

So the witnesses see the offerings of the feast of trumpets piled upon the regular sacrifices of the day, not replacing but adding to them. The witnesses note the accumulation: “beside the burnt offering of the month… and the daily burnt offering” — the special victims of the feast (a bullock, a ram, and seven lambs) were offered over and above two distinct standing sacrifices: the burnt offering of the new moon (for this first day of the month was also a new moon, with its own appointed offering, Numbers 28:11), and the continual daily burnt offering of morning and evening. They mark Ellicott’s and Wesley’s clarification that “the burnt offering of the month” means the offering of the new moon, belonging to every new moon. They observe the threefold worship of the day the witnesses count: the daily offering, the new-moon offering, and the feast offering, all rendered on the one day; the trumpets-day, falling on the new moon, gathered the most abundant sacrifice. They note the principle the witnesses see throughout: that the extraordinary offerings of the feasts did not set aside the ordinary, but were added to them; God’s due was rendered in full, the standing worship continuing while the festal was heaped upon it. They observe the fitness of so rich an offering at the opening of the holiest month; the day was honoured with sacrifice upon sacrifice, all a sweet savour to God. They note that abundance of mercy calls for abundance of worship. The verse thus adds the day’s offerings upon the others — the day of atonement following in the next verses.
7:
On Numbers 29:7 — “And ye shall have on the tenth day of this seventh month an holy convocation; and ye shall afflict your souls: ye shall not do any work therein” — the witnesses mark the day of atonement.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott refer to the fuller law of the day in Leviticus 16:1 and Leviticus 23:26. The Reformed Calvin: this was the day of atonement; though they never came before God without seeking pardon, then in a special manner they confessed their sins, a fast appointed in token of guilt. Wesley: afflict your souls by fasting and compunction for sin.

The verse sets the day of atonement. The tenth day is a holy convocation of fasting and rest. The great fast of the year is appointed.

So the witnesses see the chapter turn to the day of atonement, the one fast of the sacred year. The witnesses note the holiness of the day: “an holy convocation… ye shall not do any work therein” — the tenth of Tisri was kept as a day of total rest and sacred assembly, like the weekly Sabbath. They mark the demand peculiar to it: “ye shall afflict your souls” — Wesley explains this as fasting, abstinence from all delightful things, and compunction of heart for sin and for the judgments of God deserved or felt; the only day on which Israel was commanded to fast. They observe Calvin’s discernment of its meaning: though the people never came into God’s presence without seeking pardon, on this day they in a special manner confessed their sins, the appointed fast being a token of their guilt and humbling. They note the witnesses’ reference to the fuller law of the day given in Leviticus — the high priest’s entering the holiest with blood, the two goats, the great atonement (Leviticus 16:1; Leviticus 23:26); here only the added burnt offering is prescribed. They observe the place of the day in the month: between the trumpets that summoned to repentance and the tabernacles that crowned the year with joy stood the solemn fast of atonement — first the call, then the cleansing, then the rejoicing. They note the typology the witnesses press: the day of atonement is the clearest figure of Christ’s expiation, the afflicted soul answering the repentance that receives him. The verse thus sets the day of atonement — its burnt offering following in the next verses.
8:
On Numbers 29:8 — “But ye shall offer a burnt offering unto the LORD for a sweet savour; one young bullock, one ram, and seven lambs of the first year” — the witnesses mark the burnt offering of the atonement day.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: a burnt offering for a sweet savour, one young bullock, one ram, and seven lambs without blemish, with their meat offering. The Reformed Gill: the same as on the first day; Aben Ezra thinks the ram here different from that of the atonement service.

The verse names the offering. A bullock, a ram, and seven lambs are offered on the atonement day. The fast is attended by sacrifice.

So the witnesses see a burnt offering appointed for the day of atonement, the same in kind as that of the feast of trumpets. The witnesses note the victims: “one young bullock, one ram, and seven lambs of the first year” — offered whole to God as a burnt offering for a sweet savour, with their meat offering of flour and oil; Gill observes it was the same offering as on the first day of the month. They mark the distinction the witnesses keep: this burnt offering was over and above the great sin offering of the day — the bullock and the two goats by which the high priest made atonement within the veil (Leviticus 16:1); the festal burnt offering here prescribed was additional to that proper atonement service. They observe Gill’s note of Aben Ezra’s opinion that the ram offered here was a different ram from that of the atonement ritual, the offerings being distinct. They note the fitness the witnesses see: that even on the day of fasting and humbling, a burnt offering of entire consecration ascended to God; the affliction of the soul was joined to the offering of the best of the herd and flock. They observe that atonement and consecration met on the day: the sin offering covered guilt, the burnt offering expressed devotion. They note the typology held throughout: the unblemished victims point to Christ, the perfect offering through whom alone the day’s atonement availed. They observe that the worship of the fast was full, both in penitence and in sacrifice. The verse thus names the burnt offering of the day — its meat offering following in the next verse.
9:
On Numbers 29:9 — “And their meat offering shall be of flour mingled with oil, three tenth deals to a bullock, and two tenth deals to one ram” — the witnesses mark the meat offering of the atonement day.

The Reformed Gill: the meat offering was of the same quantity of flour and oil for a bullock, a ram, and a lamb as on the first day of the month.

The verse sets the meat offering. The same measures of flour and oil accompany the atonement burnt offering. The grain offering attends the day.

So the witnesses see the meat offering of the atonement day set in the same fixed measure as on the feast of trumpets. The witnesses note the proportions: “three tenth deals to a bullock, and two tenth deals to one ram” (with a tenth for each lamb) — the meat offering of fine flour mingled with oil, measured to each victim, the largest for the bullock, less for the ram, least for each lamb. They mark Gill’s observation that this was the same quantity of flour and oil as on the first day of the month; the rule of the accompanying offering was uniform, the grain offering always proportioned to the animal it attended. They observe the principle the witnesses have traced through the chapter: that the burnt offering was never offered alone, but with its meat offering, the fruits of the field joined to the firstlings of the flock and herd; even on the day of fasting, when the people themselves ate nothing, the offering of bread and oil was brought to God. They note the meaning the witnesses find: the fine flour and oil owned God as the giver of all sustenance, and pointed to Christ, the bread of life. They observe the fitness that, on the day the people afflicted their souls and abstained from food, the meal offering still ascended to God; their fast was not a withholding from him but a humbling before him. They note that the worship of the day was furnished in full. The verse thus sets the meat offering — the sin offering of the day following in the next verses.
11:
On Numbers 29:11 — “One kid of the goats for a sin offering; beside the sin offering of atonement… and the continual burnt offering” — the witnesses mark the sin offering of the day, besides the great atonement.

The Reformed Gill: this kid, different from the goat of the atonement service, was offered after it; the Jewish writers note the goat offered without, though of the same kind, was distinct from that whose blood was brought within.

The verse adds a sin offering. A kid is offered, besides the great atonement sacrifice. Atonement is heaped upon atonement.

So the witnesses see a sin offering of a kid appointed for the day, over and above the great sin offering of the atonement itself. The witnesses note the careful distinction Gill draws out: “one kid of the goats for a sin offering; beside the sin offering of atonement” — this kid was a sin offering distinct from, and additional to, the goat by which the high priest made the special atonement of the day; the Jewish writers observe that the goat offered “without,” in the ordinary way, though of the same kind, was a different victim from the goat whose blood was carried within the veil into the holiest (Leviticus 16:15). They mark the order Gill notes, that this added sin offering was offered after the proper atonement service. They observe the heaping of atonement upon atonement the witnesses see in this: on the very day of the great expiation, with its unique entering of the holiest, yet another sin offering was required; the day was full of the confession of sin and the shedding of atoning blood, that the guilt of the people might be wholly covered. They note the standing offerings still beneath all: “and the continual burnt offering” — the daily sacrifice went on, the festal offerings added upon it. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: that the sense of sin and the need of atonement were pressed home most of all on the day of atonement; the more solemn the day, the more abundant the sin offering. They note the typology: every sin offering, the great and the added, points to Christ, made sin for us (2 Corinthians 5:21). The verse thus adds the sin offering of the day — the feast of tabernacles following in the next verses.
12:
On Numbers 29:12 — “And on the fifteenth day of the seventh month ye shall have an holy convocation… and ye shall keep a feast unto the LORD seven days” — the witnesses mark the feast of tabernacles, the joy after the fast.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: a solemn assembly, and for seven days sacrifices, thirteen bullocks the first day, one fewer each day. Charles Ellicott: a larger number of burnt offerings than for any other festival; seventy oxen in all over the seven days. The Puritan Henry: soon after the day of affliction came the feast of rejoicing; those that sow in tears reap in joy. The Reformed Calvin: the chief of their festivals, dwelling in tabernacles seven days. Wesley: while we are in these tabernacles, we must offer sacrifices.

The verse sets the feast of tabernacles. The fifteenth day begins seven days of holy joy. The gladdest feast follows the solemn fast.

So the witnesses see the feast of tabernacles open, the gladdest of the feasts, following hard upon the fast of atonement. The witnesses note the joy after the affliction, which Henry draws out beautifully: soon after the day of atonement, in which the people afflicted their souls, came the feast of tabernacles, in which they were to rejoice before the LORD; for those that sow in tears shall reap in joy (Psalm 126:5); the cleansing past, the people kept festival. They mark the eminence of the feast: Calvin calls it the chief of their festivals, in which they dwelt in tabernacles (booths) for seven days; Wesley notes that all the days of their dwelling in booths they must offer sacrifices, and draws the lesson that while we are in these earthly tabernacles we too must offer to God. They observe the abundance of the offerings, which the witnesses count: Clarke and Ellicott note that this feast had a larger number of burnt offerings than any other — thirteen bullocks on the first day, one fewer each succeeding day, with rams and lambs, so that seventy oxen in all were offered over the seven days. They note the joy the feast commemorated: the dwelling in booths recalled the wilderness keeping, and the ingathering of harvest crowned the year with thanksgiving. They observe the typology the witnesses see: the feast of God tabernacling with his people looks on to the Word made flesh, who dwelt (tabernacled) among us (John 1:14). They note that joy followed atonement in God’s appointed order. The verse thus sets the feast of tabernacles — its great burnt offering following in the next verse.
13:
On Numbers 29:13 — “And ye shall offer a burnt offering… thirteen young bullocks, two rams, and fourteen lambs of the first year; they shall be without blemish” — the witnesses mark the great burnt offering of the first day of tabernacles.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: thirteen young bullocks, two rams, and fourteen lambs without blemish, with their meat offering. The Reformed Gill: the burnt offering of the first of the seven days, the greatest of any feast.

The verse names the great offering. Thirteen bullocks, two rams, fourteen lambs are offered the first day. The richest sacrifice of the year begins.

So the witnesses see the burnt offering of the first day of tabernacles, the most abundant of any feast in the year. The witnesses note the multitude of victims: “thirteen young bullocks, two rams, and fourteen lambs of the first year” — offered whole to God on the first day, with their meat and drink offerings; far more than the single bullock of the other feasts. They mark the descending number that the witnesses count through the seven days: thirteen bullocks the first day, twelve the second, and so on, one fewer each day, down to seven on the last, making seventy oxen in all over the week — the great and diminishing offering peculiar to this feast. They observe the requirement of perfection: “they shall be without blemish” — every one of the many victims must be sound and unflawed, fit for God’s altar. They note the fitness the witnesses see in so lavish an offering at the gladdest feast: the joy of the ingathering and the wilderness remembrance overflowed in the most abundant sacrifice of the year; the more the people rejoiced, the more they brought to God. They observe the typology the witnesses carry: the unblemished victims, in such number, point to the fulness of the sacrifice of Christ, more than sufficient for all. They note that the feast of joy was the feast of fullest offering. They observe that the great number set this feast apart from all the rest. The verse thus names the great burnt offering of the first day — the offerings of the following days continuing the descending count.
35:
On Numbers 29:35 — “On the eighth day ye shall have a solemn assembly: ye shall do no servile work therein” — the witnesses mark the eighth-day solemn assembly.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: azereth, “solemn assembly,” better “closing feast day” (Leviticus 23:36; Nehemiah 8:18). Adam Clarke: among the Jews this was esteemed the chief or high day of the feast, though fewer sacrifices were offered. The Reformed Gill: the day after the seven days, not properly part of the feast but an appendage to it.

The verse adds the eighth day. A solemn assembly closes the feast, with rest from work. The great closing day crowns the season.

So the witnesses see the feast of tabernacles closed with a solemn assembly on the eighth day. The witnesses note its distinct character: “on the eighth day ye shall have a solemn assembly” — Gill observes that this day, the day after the seven, was not properly a part of the feast of tabernacles (the booth-dwelling being for the seven days only) but a sort of appendage or closing festival joined to it. They mark the word Ellicott unfolds: azereth, here rendered “solemn assembly,” better “closing feast day,” the same term used in Leviticus and Nehemiah of this concluding day. They observe Clarke’s striking note that among the Jews this eighth day was esteemed the chief or high day of the whole feast, though fewer sacrifices were offered on it than on the others; the people seem to have finished the solemnity with a greater measure of joy and devotion, gathering up the gladness of the week into its close. They note the rest commanded: “ye shall do no servile work therein” — like the first day, the eighth was a holy day of rest and worship, framing the feast at its end as the first had at its beginning. They observe the New Testament connection the witnesses see: this “great day of the feast” is the day on which our Lord stood and cried, “If any man thirst, let him come unto me, and drink” (John 7:37); the closing day of joy pointed to him. They note that the eighth day, beyond the seven, may figure the eternal rest and joy beyond this world’s pilgrimage. The verse thus adds the eighth-day assembly — its burnt offering following in the next verse.
36:
On Numbers 29:36 — “But ye shall offer a burnt offering… one bullock, one ram, seven lambs of the first year without blemish” — the witnesses mark the burnt offering of the eighth day.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: a burnt offering, one bullock, one ram, seven lambs without blemish, with their meat and drink offerings. The Reformed Gill: the same as on the first and tenth days, with a goat for a sin offering.

The verse names the eighth-day offering. One bullock, one ram, seven lambs are offered. The closing day returns to the simpler sacrifice.

So the witnesses see the burnt offering of the eighth day, fewer in number than the seven days before, returning to the measure of the other feasts. The witnesses note the victims: “one bullock, one ram, seven lambs of the first year without blemish” — Gill observes this was the same offering as on the first day of the month (trumpets) and the tenth (atonement), with its meat and drink offerings and a goat for a sin offering; the eighth day fell back from the lavish thirteen-to-seven bullocks of the feast week to a single bullock. They mark the contrast the witnesses note: through the seven days of tabernacles the bullocks descended from thirteen to seven, a great and diminishing offering; but on the eighth day, the closing assembly, only one bullock was offered, as on the other solemn days. They observe that, though Clarke noted the eighth day was esteemed the chief day, its sacrifices were the fewer; the greatness of the day lay not in the number of victims but in the joy and solemnity of the closing assembly. They note the requirement of perfection still: “without blemish” — the victims, though few, must be sound and fit for God. They observe the typology held throughout: the unblemished offering points to Christ, the one perfect sacrifice. They note that the feast closed, as the other feasts were kept, with the simpler burnt offering of consecration. They observe that the worship of the eighth day was full though fewer in victims. The verse thus names the burnt offering of the eighth day — the whole list summed in the next verses.
39:
On Numbers 29:39 — “These things ye shall do unto the LORD in your set feasts, beside your vows, and your freewill offerings” — the witnesses mark the appointed offerings distinguished from the voluntary.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: better, “these sacrifices shall ye offer at your set seasons”; the sacrifices of this chapter were appointed independently of the vows and freewill offerings. The Reformed Gill: “in your set feasts,” rather the fixed and appointed times, as the Jewish writers interpret it.

The verse sums the appointed offerings. These are besides the vows and freewill gifts. The fixed and the voluntary are distinguished.

So the witnesses see the whole list of festal offerings summed up and distinguished from the people’s voluntary sacrifices. The witnesses note the comprehensive close: “these things ye shall do unto the LORD in your set feasts” — Ellicott would render it “these sacrifices shall ye offer at your set seasons”; Gill notes that “set feasts” means rather the fixed and appointed times, since not all were feast days (the day of atonement was a fast); the verse gathers up all the stated offerings prescribed for the appointed seasons of the year. They mark the distinction drawn: “beside your vows, and your freewill offerings” — these appointed, public, calendar sacrifices were over and above whatever a man might offer privately in payment of a vow, or bring of his own free devotion; the fixed worship of the nation did not absorb or replace the voluntary worship of the individual, but stood beside it. They observe the order the witnesses see: God appointed a settled round of public sacrifice for the set seasons, binding on all; yet he left room for the freewill gifts of love and the offerings of vows, that devotion might both keep the appointed times and overflow them. They note the lesson: that the stated worship of God is the duty of all, and is not lessened by, nor does it crowd out, the free offerings of a willing heart. They observe that the chapter’s long list of appointed sacrifices is here rounded off and bounded. They note that public order and private devotion both have their place. The verse thus sums the appointed offerings — Moses’ faithful telling of them closing in the next verse.
40:
On Numbers 29:40 — “And Moses told the children of Israel according to all that the LORD commanded Moses” — the witnesses mark Moses’ faithful declaring of the offerings.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: this verse forms the conclusion of the list of sacrifices in chapters 28 and 29. Charles Ellicott: in the Hebrew Bible this verse begins the thirtieth chapter. The Reformed Gill: Moses repeated all these laws to the people, being a faithful servant.

The verse closes the list. Moses tells Israel all that the LORD commanded. The faithful mediator delivers the whole.

So the witnesses see the long list of appointed sacrifices closed with Moses’ faithful declaring of them to the people. The witnesses note the obedience: “Moses told the children of Israel according to all that the LORD commanded Moses” — Gill observes that Moses repeated to the people all these laws concerning the sacrifices, with their additions and explanations, being a faithful servant who delivered his Lord’s whole message, holding nothing back. They mark Keil and Delitzsch’s note that this verse forms the conclusion of the great list of public sacrifices set out in chapters 28 and 29 — the daily, the sabbath, the new-moon, and all the yearly feasts; the whole calendar of Israel’s stated worship is here gathered and sealed by Moses’ declaration. They observe Ellicott’s note that in the Hebrew Bible this verse begins the thirtieth chapter, joining the close of the sacrifices to what follows. They note the faithfulness of the mediator the witnesses commend: what God spoke to Moses, Moses spoke to Israel, exactly and fully; the steward kept and delivered the trust. They observe the fitness of the close: that the people might know the whole appointed order of their worship, set down and declared, that they should serve God in his own way at his own times. They note that the chapter, a calendar of grace, was thus handed to the people to be kept. They observe that all the offerings together rehearsed, in shadow, the one offering of Christ. The verse thus closes the list: Moses tells Israel all — the law turning, in what follows, to the matter of vows.
